In \delta (transition function), there are |Q| \times |\Gamma| = 12 mappings. The number of TMs is the number of combination of different output for these 12 mappings, which is (|Q| \times |\Gamma| \times 2)^{12} = 24^{12} \times \#\{possible starting states\} \times \#\{possible accept states\}

#a. 7-tuple T = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, q_{accept}, q_{reject}). Q: set of states, \Sigma: set of variables, \Gamma: set of tape alphabets, q_0: start state, q_{accept}: accept state, q_{reject}: reject state, \delta: transition function: Q \times \Sigma^k \times \Gamma^k \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma^k \times \{R, S\}^k. #b. The same computational power. #c. A standard TM head can move left or right on the tape. It can read/write on positions it already passed. M has multiple tapes but it can only move right or stay, needs another tape to read/write on positions it already passed. Same computational power.

#a. A problem that can't be solved by Turing Machine. #b. Yes (Decidable). #c. Compare the languages generated by 2 CFGs using Turing Machine with unlimited tape alphabet. It will recursively compare 2 strings, if match marked with an alphabet, if not reject (halts). When the tape head reaches blank symbol (marked end of string) -> comparison completed -> 2 languages are similar -> accept. The machine always halts -> Decidable problem.
DFA. If the input symbol belongs to syntax error -> transition to trap state (not accepted). Otherwise if the input symbol is valid -> transition to accepted state (accepted).
